---
title: "Boltz-2 as a TCRdock backend replacement"
subtitle: "Tool evaluation · Issue #188 · 2026-05-27"
author: "Jin-Ho Lee"
institute: "Splice Neoepitope Pipeline · JH M Lee Lab"
date: 2026-05-27
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, ../../slides/custom.scss]
    footer: "Splice Neoepitope Pipeline · JH M Lee Lab"
    incremental: false
    slide-number: c/t
    progress: true
    chalkboard: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    fig-align: center
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
# PDF (beamer) render disabled — same Quarto/pandoc longtable issue as the pilot deck.
# HTML reveal.js is the primary delivery; "Print → Save as PDF" in Chrome for handouts.
bibliography: refs.bib
csl: ../../slides/nature.csl
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## The question

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 2.2em; font-weight: bold; line-height: 1.2; margin-top: 0.4em;"}
Should Boltz-2 replace\
TCRdock's AF-Multimer\
backend?
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Why now:** parent [Issue #432](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/432) AC requires a per-scorer integration verdict — **(a)** Modeling sub-issue under [milestone 29](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/milestone/29) **or (b)** decline-with-rationale — for each of Boltz-2 (this Issue), HERMES ([Issue #218](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/218), → a), ImmSET ([Issue #201](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/201), → b), t2pmhc/TCRLens ([Issue #236](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/236)), and the [Issue #316](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/316) outcome.
:::

---

## The tool — Boltz-2 at a glance {.smaller}

**Boltz-2** — Passaro et al., **bioRxiv** 2025-06-18 [@passaro2025boltz2].

::: {.incremental}
- **Architecture:** co-folding biomolecular interaction model; predicts structure + binding affinity in one pass. Same family as AlphaFold-3 / Chai-1 / RosettaFold-AllAtom.
- **Training:** explicitly includes **MHC-peptide and MHC-peptide-TCR complexes** — Class I sequences cropped 180 residues, ≤100 sequences/allele.
- **Speed claim:** ~**1000× faster than FEP** for small-molecule-protein affinity prediction — *not* a structure-prediction speedup vs TCRdock.
- **Code + weights:** [`jwohlwend/boltz`](https://github.com/jwohlwend/boltz) — **MIT-licensed**, no Dropbox-hosted parameter URLs (vs TCRdock's AlphaFold params).
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**The optimistic case (entering this eval):** modern co-folding successor to TCRdock's aging AF-Multimer wrapper; explicit TCR-pMHC training data; open license; sidesteps the P100/Pascal compatibility risk on TCRdock's pinned CUDA stack.
:::

---

## Why it works (and where it doesn't) {.smaller}

**Mechanism:** Boltz-2 is a **co-folding diffusion model** — joint MSA + pairwise representation feeds a diffusion backbone that denoises atom coordinates while a separate head predicts binding affinity. End-to-end: amino-acid sequences in, structure + affinity out, no rigid-body docking step.

::: {.incremental}
- **What carries from AlphaFold lineage:** the PDB-scale pretraining (~200k structures) teaches the model general protein-protein geometry — interface contacts, side-chain packing, backbone topology.
- **What the TCR-pMHC training adds:** a few thousand explicit complexes [@passaro2025boltz2] anchor the geometry class — the model "knows what a TCR-pMHC interface looks like" for sequences near the training set.
- **Why OOD fails:** TCR-peptide interfaces are dominated by **CDR3-peptide contacts**, the most diverse and least-conserved component. Novel CDR3 × novel peptide pairings (= splice-junction neoepitopes) sit far from any training example; the model extrapolates from interface motifs it never saw.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
**The implication:** explicit TCR-pMHC training boosts the **in-distribution** DockQ ceiling but does not close the OOD gap. This is the mechanism behind the seen-vs-unseen split on the next slide.
:::

---

## Where it would have plugged in {.smaller}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 11
flowchart LR
    A["Top neoepitope<br/>candidate<br/>(MHCflurry rank 1)"] --> B1["TCRdock<br/>(AF-Multimer v2)"]
    A --> B2["Boltz-2<br/>(co-folding)"]
    F["VDJdb TCR panel<br/>or fallback DMF5"] --> B1
    F --> B2
    B1 --> C1["PDB structure<br/>+ ipTM, pLDDT, PAE"]
    B2 --> C2["PDB structure<br/>+ affinity, pLDDT"]
    C1 --> D["HERMES + NetTCR-struc<br/>structural-QC stack"]
    C2 -.alternative.-> D

    style B2 fill:#ffe6a3,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:3px
    style B1 fill:#a3d8ff,stroke:#1e5ba8,stroke-width:2px
```

**Two integration shapes considered:**

| Shape | Description |
|---|---|
| Replacement | Boltz-2 replaces TCRdock outright (single PDB output, single confidence) |
| Parallel cross-check | Run both, use TCRdock↔Boltz-2 structural agreement as a QC signal |

---

## The OOD generalization gap {.smaller}

[@lu2025tcrpmhcbench] benchmark 6 tools (AF2, AF3, Boltz-2, Chai-1, TCRmodel2, tFold-TCR) on 20 experimentally resolved Class I TCR-pMHC complexes.

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Boltz-2 DockQ split:**

| Cohort | DockQ | CAPRI band |
|---|---|---|
| Seen in training | **~0.91** | high |
| Unseen complexes | **~0.70** | medium |

**Splice neoepitope peptides are OOD by construction** — junction-spanning peptide sequences don't appear in any TCR-pMHC training set. Our actual workload sits in the **0.70 regime**.

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
**Headline:** explicit TCR-pMHC training in Boltz-2 gives the seen-complex DockQ; it does *not* transfer cleanly to novel complexes. The 0.91 number is not the number that describes our workload.
:::

. . .

[@lu2025tcrpmhcbench] also report **AF3 best-overall** on the unseen-complex split — the better candidate for a TCRdock-backend modernization track (covered by [Issue #316](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/316)).

:::

:::

---

## Three reasons to decline {.smaller}

::: {.incremental}
1. **OOD by construction is the deal-breaker.** Splice-junction peptides don't appear in Boltz-2's training data; we'd be in the 0.70 (medium-CAPRI) regime, not the 0.91 headline. The training-set inclusion of MHC-peptide-TCR complexes doesn't help when our peptides are OOD.
2. **Parity-at-best with TCRdock, not a clear quality win.** TCRdock (AF-Multimer-backed) typically scores 0.5–0.7 DockQ on TCR-pMHC benchmarks. Boltz-2 at 0.70 OOD is comparable, not clearly better. The "1000× faster" claim in [@passaro2025boltz2] is vs **FEP for small-molecule affinity**, not vs TCRdock for structure inference.
3. **AF3 is the higher-value next experiment.** [@lu2025tcrpmhcbench] benchmark: AF3 best-overall on the unseen-complex split. If we want a TCRdock-backend modernization track, AF3 is the right target — and [Issue #316](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/316) already covers it.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Integration cost not justified:** new Docker container (different ML stack), new chain-relabel logic, new confidence-score plumbing — for parity-at-best on our actual use case.
:::

---

## Decision

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 3em; font-weight: bold; margin-top: 0.5em;"}
\(b\) Decline
:::

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.4em; margin-top: 0.5em;"}
not a TCRdock-backend replacement for our use case
:::

. . .

**(b) decline rationale posted as a comment on [Issue #188](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/188)**. No Modeling sub-issue filed under [milestone 29](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/milestone/29).

. . .

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**[Issue #432](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/432) verdict progress:** 3 of 5 scorers resolved — HERMES → (a), ImmSET → (b), **Boltz-2 → (b)**. Remaining: t2pmhc/TCRLens ([Issue #236](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/236)), [Issue #316](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/316) outcome.
:::

---

## What would change the decision {.smaller}

The (b) decline is **not permanent**. Three concrete triggers would reopen the evaluation:

::: {.incremental}
1. **A future Boltz release narrows the seen-vs-unseen gap.** If Boltz-3 or comparable model reports DockQ ≥0.85 on held-out TCR-pMHC with no peptide identity to training, the OOD argument weakens.
2. **The AF3 eval ([Issue #316](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/316)) declines.** If AF3 turns out to have its own integration blockers (licensing, weights, throughput), Boltz-2's MIT license + open weights become more attractive even at parity DockQ.
3. **Cohort-scale structure prediction becomes a hard requirement.** Currently we structure-predict only the top-1 candidate per patient. If scientific framing shifts to top-N or cohort-wide, Boltz-2's throughput advantage (regardless of FEP-vs-TCRdock framing) could justify the integration cost.
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Manuscript carry-forward to [Issue #432](https://github.com/Jin-HoMLee/splice-neoepitope-pipeline/issues/432):** Boltz-2's seen↔unseen gap sharpens the prediction-vs-QC distinction. Co-folding models trained on TCR-pMHC complexes (Boltz-2) hit a 0.91 → 0.70 generalization gap; **structural-QC scorers** (HERMES + NetTCR-struc, both (a)-integrated) are the better complement to TCRdock for our OOD splice-neoepitope workload than a second structure-prediction model.
:::

---

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
